# Layout Building Blocks (Stacks)

Welcome to SwiftUI layouts. In HTML and React, developers reach for native `<div>` elements and CSS Flexbox to physically position items on the screen. In Swift, you explicitly use **Stacks**, which are structurally safer and highly optimized pre-configured wrappers.

## The `<div>` Equivalent

**Fact:** Stacks act conceptually identically to `div` tags, but they come with pre-baked flexbox styles specifically tailored for mobile and desktop screens.
- In React, a `div` is structurally dumb. You must explicitly apply `display: flex; flex-direction: column` just to format items sequentially.
- In Swift, Stacks are inherently intelligent and are implicitly pre-configured to handle flexible layout distribution and alignment.

### 1. VStack (Vertical Stack)

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### What does this look like in React?
```javascript
// React equivalent using inline Flexbox styles
<div style={{ display: 'flex', flexDirection: 'column', alignItems: 'center', gap: '8px' }}>
    <h1>Top Item</h1>
    <p>Bottom Item</p>
</div>
```
:::

**Swift Equivalent:**
```swift
// Swift automatically stacks vertically and injects a default Apple-standard gap
VStack {
    Text("Top Item")
        .font(.title)
    Text("Bottom Item")
}
```

### 2. HStack (Horizontal Stack)

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### What does this look like in React?
```javascript
// React equivalent using inline Flexbox styles
<div style={{ display: 'flex', flexDirection: 'row', alignItems: 'center', gap: '8px' }}>
    <span>Left Item</span>
    <span>Right Item</span>
</div>
```
:::

**Swift Equivalent:**
```swift
// Swift automatically links them horizontally
HStack {
    Text("Left Item")
    Text("Right Item")
}
```

### 3. ZStack (Depth Stack)

**Fact:** `ZStack` physically stacks visual elements on top of each other strictly along the Z-axis (depth). 
- **Rule:** The first item written systematically in the code is placed at the mathematical bottom, and subsequent items visually stack sequentially on top of it.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### What does this look like in React?
```javascript
// React typically overrides standard flow using relative and absolute positioning
<div style={{ position: 'relative', width: '200px', height: '200px' }}>
    <img src="background.jpg" style={{ position: 'absolute', width: '100%', zIndex: 0 }} />
    <h1 style={{ position: 'absolute', zIndex: 10 }}>Text on top</h1>
</div>
```
:::

**Swift Equivalent:**
```swift
// No manual CSS absolute positioning required
ZStack {
    Image("background") // Bottom physical layer
        .resizable()

    Text("Text on top") // Top physical layer
        .foregroundColor(.white)
}
```

## Making Grids

**Fact:** In React, creating a responsive grid requires verbose CSS Grid definitions (`grid-template-columns`, `gap`, `fr` fractions). In Swift, wrappers like `LazyVGrid` naturally handle the complex fractional mathematics cleanly at compile-time.

**Why are they called Lazy?**
- Because they only evaluate identically when elements physically overlap the viewable screen. If you inject 10,000 items in a grid manually, Swift only physically renders the 10 that the user is currently looking at, guaranteeing flawless performance automatically.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
#### What does this look like in React?
```javascript
// React using bulky CSS Grid
<div style={{ 
    display: 'grid', 
    gridTemplateColumns: 'repeat(2, 1fr)', 
    gap: '16px' 
}}>
    <div>Item 1</div>
    <div>Item 2</div>
    <div>Item 3</div>
    <div>Item 4</div>
</div>
```
:::

**Swift Equivalent:**
```swift
// ⬇️ 1. Define the grid spacing mathematically (Two identically flexible columns)
let columns = [
    GridItem(.flexible()),
    GridItem(.flexible())
]

var body: some View {
    // ⬇️ 2. Apply it cleanly to the Lazy wrapper
    LazyVGrid(columns: columns, spacing: 16) {
        Text("Item 1")
        Text("Item 2")
        Text("Item 3")
        Text("Item 4")
    }
}
```

**Fact vs Verbosity Conclusion:** Notice exactly how much less verbose the SwiftUI wrapper visually acts. You explicitly define the mechanical structure of your layouts once as pure data (`let columns`), and inject them into the standard logical Grid components—entirely avoiding manual CSS string-map configuration.
